Inventory Management Inventory management is the backbone of any successful warehouse operation. With ASC's Manufacturing and Warehouse Inventory Management Software, businesses can harness the power of advanced inventory management tools designed to optimize stock levels, reduce carrying costs, and streamline operations. Our solution provides real-time visibility into inventory, ensuring that businesses can make informed decisions, meet customer demands, and achieve operational excellence. Whether you're a small business or a large enterprise, our Inventory Management feature offers the flexibility and scalability needed to manage inventory efficiently and effectively. Real-Time Inventory Visibility In the fast-paced world of warehousing, having real-time insights into stock levels is invaluable. That's why ASCTrac provides instant visibility into inventory across multiple locations. This real-time access ensures businesses can track stock movements, anticipate demand, and avoid stockouts or overstocking. By having a clear picture of inventory levels, businesses can make informed decisions, optimize storage space, and reduce holding costs. The ability to access this data in real-time not only streamlines operations but also enhances customer satisfaction as orders can be fulfilled more efficiently . Automated Reorder Points Balancing stock levels can be tricky. To address this, our system allows businesses to set automated reorder points. This feature ensures timely replenishment and optimal stock levels. By automating this process, businesses can reduce the risk of stockouts, ensuring continuous product availability. Additionally, by setting reorder points based on historical data and sales forecasts, businesses can maintain a lean inventory, reducing holding costs and improving cash flow. This automation also reduces the administrative burden, allowing staff to focus on other crucial tasks. Multi-Location Management Managing inventory across multiple locations can be a challenge. Thankfully, ASCTrac offers a unified view, streamlining stock transfers and providing a holistic view of inventory health. With the rise of e-commerce and global supply chains, businesses often operate in multiple locations. This feature ensures that stock levels are balanced across all locations, optimizing storage space and reducing transportation costs. Furthermore, by having a centralized system, businesses can ensure consistency in inventory records, improving accuracy and reducing discrepancies. Advanced Reporting and Analytics In today's data-driven world, insights into inventory turnover and stock aging are crucial. With this in mind, our system offers in-depth analytics. These insights empower businesses to optimize stock levels, identify slow-moving items, and make informed purchasing decisions. By analyzing sales trends, seasonality, and other factors, businesses can forecast demand more accurately, ensuring they have the right products in the right quantities at the right time. Additionally, with customizable reports , businesses can gain insights tailored to their specific needs, driving continuous improvement. Lot and Serial Number Tracking Traceability is paramount in many industries. Recognizing this, our system supports lot and serial number tracking. This feature ensures products can be traced throughout their lifecycle, from production to sale. Whether it's for regulatory compliance, warranty claims, or quality control, this level of traceability is invaluable. Furthermore, by tracking products at the lot or serial number level, businesses can quickly identify and address any issues, ensuring product quality and enhancing customer satisfaction. Cycle Counting Accuracy in inventory records is essential. That's why our ASCTrac system supports cycle counting. This feature allows for regular verification of stock levels without disruptive full-scale stock takes. By conducting frequent cycle counts, businesses can identify discrepancies early, ensuring accurate inventory records. This not only reduces the risk of stockouts or overstocking but also improves financial reporting. Additionally, by identifying the root causes of discrepancies, businesses can implement corrective actions, reducing errors and improving operational efficiency. Demand Forecasting Anticipating future inventory needs is crucial. With our advanced demand forecasting tools , businesses can proactively manage inventory based on predicted demand patterns. By analyzing historical sales data, seasonality, market trends, and other factors, these tools provide accurate forecasts, ensuring businesses have the right products in the right quantities. This proactive approach reduces the risk of stockouts, improves service levels, and reduces holding costs. Furthermore, by aligning inventory levels with demand, businesses can optimize storage space, reduce obsolescence, and improve cash flow. Integration with Procurement Seamless operations are the goal of every warehouse. To this end, as inventory levels change, our system triggers procurement processes. This integration ensures timely stock replenishment, reducing the risk of stockouts. By automating the procurement process based on inventory levels, businesses can ensure they always have the right products on hand. This not only improves service levels but also reduces administrative overheads, allowing staff to focus on other crucial tasks. Customizable SKU Management Every business has unique inventory needs. Recognizing this, ASC Software allows for customizable SKU definitions. This flexibility caters to specific business requirements, ensuring accurate tracking and reporting. Whether it's for different product variations, packaging types, or other factors, this customizable approach ensures businesses can manage their inventory effectively. Furthermore, by having a standardized SKU system, businesses can improve accuracy, streamline operations, and enhance reporting. Customizable Alerts and Notifications Staying informed is crucial. That's why our customizable alert system ensures you're always updated about crucial inventory events. Whether it's stock level changes, reorder points, or other alerts, this system ensures businesses are always in the loop. By staying informed, businesses can make proactive decisions, optimize stock levels, and enhance service levels. Returns Management Efficiently handling returns is vital for customer satisfaction. With this in mind, ASC Software's ASCTrac system streamlines the returns process. This feature ensures prompt processing and restocking, reducing the administrative burden and improving inventory turnover. By having a standardized returns process, businesses can ensure consistency, reduce errors, and enhance customer satisfaction. Furthermore, by analyzing return data, businesses can identify trends, address quality issues, and improve product offerings. Perishable Goods Management Managing perishable goods presents unique challenges. To address this, our system offers tailored functionalities. These features ensure items are managed based on their expiry dates, reducing waste and ensuring product quality. Whether it's for food products , pharmaceuticals , or other perishable items, this feature is invaluable. By tracking expiry dates, businesses can ensure they sell products in the right order, reducing obsolescence and improving profitability. Role-Based Access Control Data security is paramount. That's why ASCTrac offers role-based access control. This feature ensures that only authorized personnel can access sensitive inventory data. By defining roles and permissions, businesses can ensure data integrity, reduce the risk of errors, and enhance security. Whether it's for managers, warehouse staff, or other personnel, this customizable approach ensures everyone has access to the data they need, and nothing more. Integration with Sales and Distribution Consistency across operations is key. With this in mind, as sales orders are processed, our system adjusts inventory levels automatically. This integration ensures accuracy, reduces the risk of stockouts, and improves service levels. By aligning inventory and sales processes, businesses can ensure they always have the right products on hand, enhancing customer satisfaction and reducing lead times. Mobile Inventory Management Efficiency and accuracy are paramount in inventory management. With this in mind, our system integrates seamlessly with mobile, barcode, RF and RFID technologies. These integrations enhance inventory operations, ensuring accurate tracking, streamlined receiving and dispatching, and reduced errors. By leveraging these technologies, businesses can improve productivity, reduce administrative overheads, and enhance customer satisfaction. Inventory Management is designed to meet the dictates of market place and support the company's Strategic Plan. Do you know that Inventory is the soul of all organization's supply chains? Inventory management is crucial for a business to understand its inventory and execute efficient, cost-effective operations; Effective inventory management reduces costs, which further keeps accounts and finances under control. The survival of an organisation now depends on its capacity to manage inventory. If you don't have good control over your inventory, the day is not far when you will lose control of your profits. To grow earnings and survive in the competitive business environment, many companies today need to change their inventory management techniques and inventory control practices. Additionally, because inventory management is one of the most demanding careers in today's market, it will assist you comprehend how and why you could decide to pursue it as a career. Inventory impacts all operating functions like manufacturing, warehousing, sales etc. Opening and closing inventory quantity should be adequate so that other business activities are not adversely affected. Thus, inventory plays an important role in the operations of an organisation. LEARNING OUTCOMES After completing this lesson the Learner z explains the meaning of inventory and its role in business; z relates the fundamental concepts of inventory; z identifies the need to manage inventory; z demonstrates various functions of inventory management; z explains the basics of the inventory control process. 1.1 MEANING OF INVENTORY Manufacturing and trading organisations keep stocks of materials for production or trading. Service organisations, like Banks, also keep stocks of the materials essential for providing services. The materials kept by organisations are collectively known as Inventories. The inventories are procured for day-to-day business processes of an organisation. Lack of adequate quantity of inventories may have an adverse affect on the business processes, eventually resulting in loss of business. 1 INVENTORY – AN INTRODUCTION Inventory – An Introduction 2 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes Fig. 1.1: Inventory Organisations may need tons of inventories starting from high-value capital goods like machinery to low-value consumables like cleaning cloths. An organisation needs to decide on the quantity of each material to be stocked so that the business processes are not affected. The process of fixing particular quantities for every material, purchasing them, holding them, issuing them for business processes, and replenishing them are collectively known as Inventory Management. INTEXT QUESTIONS 1.1 1. Define inventory. 2. Give examples of high-value inventories and low-value inventories. 1.2 ROLE OF INVENTORY IN THE PRODUCTION/TRADING PROCESSES Production or trading cannot take place without an inventory. A stationery shop needs to hold an adequate quantum of stocks like paper, notebooks, pencils, pens, etc. for trading. The shop will lose business if it doesn't have the stock of a particular stationery item a customer wants to buy. Hence, the shop keeper needs to be vigilant to procure and keep the stationery items at the appropriate quantity to serve all stationery customers. Suppose if the inventory is not there, then what would happen? Demand occurs at instant of time or during a period of time; that means, say the demand of 10 units 3 Inventory – An Introduction Notes Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types has occurred at this point in time. So, if there is a demand and the trader needs to meet the demand, the trader needs have a stock of the inventory. Suppose there is a demand of 5 units at any instant of time. So, in order to meet the demand at the same point in time, the trader needs to possess 5 units of a particular item. Fig. 1.2: Trading Processes Similarly, a manufacturing organisation uses multiple raw materials, consumables, and fuel to keep the production process going. The production may stop if a particular raw material is unavailable on a day. Stoppage of production for want of an inventory is a bad thing that can happen to a manufacturing company as this will result in business losses. INTEXT QUESTIONS 1.2 1. What will happen if inventory is not available in a shop? 2. To keep the production running, what is required? 1.3 THE NEED FOR MANAGING THE INVENTORY A business without its product is worthless. But carrying the product involves a lot of capital. Capital being a scarce resource, it would be better for the company to efficiently manage its inventory to save cost. Inventory – An Introduction 4 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes Fig. 1.3: Inventory Management Aids 1. Save on storage costs: The cost of warehousing continues to fluctuate according to how much inventory is being processed and for how long. The longer an item is sitting on a shelf without being sold, the higher the expense of a market. Proper inventory management results in products that spend less time in the warehouse before delivery. And this means lower cost of processing them. 2. Avoid spoiled or dead stock: This is not just the cost of production that a company may lose revenue from bad inventory control. Perishable products will be discarded entirely at one time if too much is shipped or if it is not processed correctly. So much stock that is 'finished' due to out of season or trend is likewise inefficient. Excellent warehouse handling helps prevent wasting resources on the overly expired or dead stock. 3. Improve cash flow: Any inventory would have been paid out in advance. Yet it's just a void in the bank balance, and a dip in cash flow, before this stock is finished. Proper handling of inventory ensures the cash is not wasted at any point.. It leaves more money in the budget to spend on production than stocktaking. 5 Inventory – An Introduction Notes Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's TypesInventory management is the basic building block for sustainability. If the product is well maintained, the rest of the supply chain strategy will work without any problem. However without proper management mistakes such as mis-shipments, out of stocks, overstocks, mis-picks, etc. might occur repeatedly. Proper maintenance of warehouses is essential. Mis-picks result from incorrect paper pick lists, disorganised shelf labels, or generally just a messy warehouse. Mis-shipments are a direct consequence of mis-picks at the outset of the production process and a lack of quality management procedures. Out of stock and overstock occurs when a firm uses manual methods to place orders without fully understanding the state of its inventory. These might not be the right product estimating indicators and could result in too much or too little stock. Not only will avoiding all these mistakes save time, but this will also help in saving energy that would be spent in rectifying the errors. When one doesn't incorporate control techniques, the chance of mistakes with people rises by the minute. And the consumer ratings and loyalties are still getting a disappointing blow. The importance of the inventory cannot be overstated for any goods-based company, which is why inventory management enhances both operating efficiency and profitability. From SMBs to businesses that already use enterprise resource planning (ERP), without a smart solution, one will face a variety of problems, including blown-out prices, lack of income, lousy customer service, and even outright disappointment. 4. Clear visibility helps in: (i) Reduce costs (ii) Optimise fulfillment (iii) Provide better customer service (iv) Prevent loss from theft, spoilage, and returns Through a broader perspective, asset management also offers visibility into the financial situation, customer habits and desires, product and market prospects, future developments, etc. Inventory – An Introduction 6 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes The feedback we have received from small business owners over the past 15 + years have been impressive as they ask about the value of inventory control for their companies. Some may get it, but some don't know exactly how many dollars their fingers dropped. Can one imagine attempting to run a company where one doesn't know how many widgets inventory contains? Or when one needs them to finish a deal, don't know exactly where they are? Biggest operating expense The importance of correct inventory management can't be stressed enough. If necessary, a decent inventory system can offer high visibility of all things, right down to the shelf and bin in multiple locations. It'll forestall stockouts, allow ample lead-time to make full stock, and provides concept of the worth occupied in the stock at any given time. For many businesses, physical inventory is their most significant expense. Properly managing this expense may mean the distinction between success and failure. One should test themselves if inventory transfers between locations are quickly, easily, and accurately recorded. The answer can reveal heaps regarding how well the system is operating. Clear visibility of inventory helps in? 1.4 FUNDAMENTALS OF INVENTORY MANAGEMENT Inventory management could be a discipline primarily regarding specifying the stocked goods' form and placement. Need of inventory management might occur at totally different locations inside a facility or inside different are as of a provider network to precede the regular and planned course of production and stock of materials. “The right stock, at the proper levels, within the right place, at the proper time, and value.” Inventory management refers to ordering, storing, and using a company's inventory. These embrace the management of raw materials, components, and finished products. 7 Inventory – An Introduction Notes Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Fig. 1.4: The Right Stock, at the Proper Levels For firms with advanced supply chains and producing processes, equalising the risks of inventory gluts and shortages is particularly troublesome. To attain these balances, companies have developed two primary strategies for inventory management: Just-in-time (JIT) and Material Requirements Planning (MRP). As a section of the supply chain, inventory management includes aspects like dominant and overseeing purchases from suppliers and customers — maintaining the storage of stock, dominant logic for the quantity of product purchasable, and order fulfillment. Naturally, the company's precise inventory management, which can vary, supported the categories of product one sells and the channels one sells them through. However, if those essential ingredients are introduced, one will have a solid foundation to create upon. Small-to-medium businesses (SMBs) typically use Google Sheets, Microsoft Excel, or different manual tools to maintain track of inventory databases thus helping them develop choices regarding orders. For these schemes, inventory control practices expand to the other side of fundamental rearrangement and stock tracking. They include everything from end- to-end output and market operations to forecasting time and demand to measurements, reporting and even accounting. Retail is the broadest catch-all term to explain business-to-consumer (B2C) marketing. There are primarily two forms of retail separated by, however, and wherever a purchase occurs. Inventory – An Introduction 8 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes (a) First, on-line retail (eCommerce) wherever the acquisition takes place digitally. (b) Second, offline retail wherever the acquisition is physical through a brick- and-mortar front or a salesman. On the other hand, Wholesale refers to business-to-business (B2B) trade. Knowing the variations and best practices of retail and wholesale are essential to success. Likewise, most businesses maintain stocks across multiple channels, and in various locations. The range of retail inventory management adds to its intricacy. It drives home its importance to one's brand inventory management in terms of the oversight of non-capitalised assets (inventory) and stock things. As an aspect of supply chain management, inventory control manages the movement of goods from suppliers to warehouses and from the facilities to the distribution location. The critical behavior of inventory management is to have an elaborated record of every new or returned product that enters or leaves a warehouse or area of sale. Inventory management is very important and is still part of a large investment by an organization till the time the goods are sold. Many expenditures are tied to inventory management, and thus a business should store, track, and ensure proper management of inventory. Overall, best practices in inventory management involve sound procuring plans to ensure goods are accessible when required while not having too few or too many resources and necessary tools to capture existing inventory. Inventory management merely refers to the handling and regulating a company's non-capitalised assets. For many retailers, this involves the overseeing and supervising of finished ready to sale goods The fundamental goal is to keep inventory levels balanced while not ever having excessive or deficient products in reserve. Therefore ordering, estimating and storing become the vital components of excellent quality inventory management. A thing that most businesses can use for quality inventory management applications. Fig. 1.5: Productivity (a) High Profitability: The software system additionally manages sales and production helping in increasing business profitability. With the power to robotise key business operations there's the chance to fulfil tasks expeditiously and accurately like managing stock levels, change listings across all sales channels and process orders which can, in turn, minimise expenses and maximise profit. Inventory – An Introduction 10 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes Fig. 1.6: Profitablity (b) Evade stock-outs and excess stock: The Inventory Management application offers multi-level stock management. It's usually difficult to manage and maintain the precise balance in inventories. Insufficient stock will cause a stock-out, mislead the customer, and cause an attainable loss, whereas excess stock will take up the warehouse area. Either way, it's an extra expense to the business. An Inventory Management application tracks low stock levels and sets automatic re-ordering for every product. It can also be set to re-order points while estimating product demand, reducing the danger of excess ordering. Fig. 1.7: Avoid Stockouts and Excess Inventory 11 Inventory – An Introduction Notes Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types(c) Quality administration: The Inventory Management application identifies, and tracks varied problems that will occur like delayed shipments, damaged packages, etc. and through analytics provides steerage concerning the factors impacting quality. Fig. 1.9: Stabilize Demand and Supply (e) Inventory reports: The application can auto generate reports. One will receive low-stock reports, perpetual inventory validation reports, and inventory forecast reports. (f) Planning and forecasting: One can determine the viability of establishing multiple regional warehouses situated close to key customers to extend efficiencies and improve service levels through insights provided by the Inventory Management application. Inventory – An Introduction 12 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes (g) Comprehensive solution: It is a key feature of an acknowledged Inventory Management application to incorporate order management, listing management practicality and shipping management and can assist in streamlining the complete sales method. (h) Tracking serial-number: Another function of the Inventory Management application is serial-number trailing. The application manages a limitless quantity of serial numbers from when the inventory is received and when it is issued. The information is recorded for good within the system for additional reference. INTEXT QUESTIONS 1.5 1. LEARNING OUTCOMES After completing this lesson the Learner z defines raw materials; z evolves the importance of raw materials; z applies the concept of work-in-progress; z describes the meaning of finished goods; 2.1 MEANING AND RELEV ANCE OF RAW MATERIALS IN THE MANUFACTURING PROCESS 1. Raw Materials Materials or substances used in the primary production or manufacturing of things are raw materials. Raw materials are commodities that are purchased and sold on global commodity exchanges. Because raw materials, like labour and capital, are factors of production, traders buy and sell them in the factor market. Raw materials are utilized in a wide 2 INVENTORY AND IT'S TYPES Inventory and it's Types 20 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes range of goods and come in various shapes and sizes. The input products or inventories that a company needs to make its products are raw materials. The steel used in car manufacturing, for example, is a raw material for an automobile manufacturer. Raw materials inventory requires precise budgeting and a unique structure for accounting relevant for manufacturing enterprises' balance sheet and income statement. Fig. 2.1: The Manufacturing Process 2. Definition and explanation of the term Let's have a look at some formal raw material definitions. Oxford Dictionary defines raw material as “The base material from which a product is manufactured”. However, for the purpose of fundamental comprehension, that is a simple way to state things. Wikipedia, on the other hand, goes into greater detail about raw materials. “A raw material, also known as a feed stock, unprocessed material, or main commodity, is a basic substance used to manufacture goods, completed products, energy, or intermediate materials that are utilized as feed stock for future finished products.” INTEXT QUESTIONS 2.1 1. (a) planning (b) forecasting (c) budgeting (d) none of the above 2.2 TYPES OF RAW MATERIALS Even though all raw materials are obtained organically, they can be classified into three groups based on their origin. 1. Plant/tree-based: Plants and trees provide raw materials such as vegetables, fruits, flowers, timber, resin, and latex. 2. Animal-based: animal-based raw materials include leather, flesh, bones, milk, wool, and silk. 3. Mining-based: minerals, metals, crude oil, coal, and other resources are mined from the soil. Fig. 2.2: Raw Material Classification Based on Origin Inventory and it's Types 22 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes 1. A production unit, on the other hand, splits raw materials into two categories. (i) Direct raw materials Direct raw materials are the primary elements used to create a final product. For example, wood is a direct raw material from which furniture like chair, tables, bed, etc. are made. Another example is leather used for making purses, shoes, bags, etc. (ii) Indirect raw materials Indirect raw materials, on the other hand, are resources that are utilized to complement direct raw materials in the production of a finished product. For example, glue, nails, varnish, and other materials needed in the construction of wooden furniture such as chairs, tables, and beds are all indirect raw materials. Buckles, metal hoops, zips, adhesive, lining fabric, colors, and other components used in the manufacture of leather purses, shoes, and bags are also indirect raw materials. Inventory management enters the scene at this point. Since the finished items we sell are manufactured from these components, it is critical to keep raw materials inventory in the proper number and location to avoid catastrophes. Fig. 2.4: Significance of Raw Materials Raw Materials Calculation Formula Now, let us look at some important formulas for calculating raw materials. To know how much raw material is applied in the manufacture process, we can use the below formula is as follows. Inventory and it's Types 24 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes Opening raw materials worth – Rs 12000 Raw materials purchased worth – Rs 5000 Closing raw materials – Rs 9000 So let us calculate as per the above formula, 12000 + 5000 – 9000 = 8000 Thereby, when we apply the formula the outcome will be the raw material worth Rs 8000 were used in the process. X Manufacturing Company. 1. Accounting Treatment of Direct and Indirect Raw Materials Raw materials can be separated into two categories in some cases: direct and indirect. Where a raw material is presented on the balance sheet and how it is expensed on the income statement depends on whether it is direct or indirect. (i) Direct Raw Materials Direct raw materials, such as wood for a chair, are materials that companies employ directly in the creation of a finished product. Direct raw materials are held in current assets and expensed in cost of goods sold on the income statement. Manufacturing firms must also go above and beyond non-manufacturing firms in terms of creating more precise expense reporting on expenses of goods supplied. Because the amount needed depends on the quantity being produced, direct raw materials are often considered variable expenses. Inventory and it's Types 25 Notes Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's TypesTo avoid shortages, a firm calculates the amount of direct raw materials it will require for specific periods. An entity can eliminate needless inventory stock, potentially lower ordering costs, and lessen the danger of material obsolescence by closely controlling the amount of direct raw materials acquired and consumed. For a variety of causes, raw materials might decay in storage or become worthless in a product. The corporation labels them obsolete in this situation. (ii) Indirect Raw Materials Indirect raw materials are not included in the final product but are used extensively throughout the manufacturing process . Long-term assets will be recorded for indirect raw materials. Within long-term assets, they might be classified as selling, general, and administrative (SG&A) or property, plant, and equipment (PPE) (PP&E). Long-term assets are frequently subject to a depreciation schedule that allows them to be expensed over time and matched to the revenue they contribute to. Depreciation for indirect raw materials will typically be shorter than for other long-term assets such as a building expensed over several years. Just imagine Shri Kumar having Furniture Manufacturing Business Direct raw materials z Timber or wood z Cushions and padding for the chairs z Cloth fabric to cover the cushions Indirect raw materials z Fittings and nails z Wood glue z Equipment for the workers, such as gloves Wood, cushioning, and fabric are considered direct raw materials because they are directly linked to the creation of the tables and chairs. The direct raw materials may be linked to each unit when determining the cost per unit. Because the amounts utilized would not be considerable, and they would not be directly linked to each unit produced, glue, nails, and worker equipment would most likely be classified indirect materials. Saudi Arabia 24.8% The World Bank uses natural resource rent to determine these percentages. After removing the costs of accessing and producing the resources, natural resource rent is the leftover revenue. Materials processing is a set of activities that transforms raw industrial materials into completed parts or products. Industrial materials, as opposed to disposable “soft” goods such as chemicals, meals, pharmaceuticals, and textiles, are utilized in the creation of “hard” goods, such as more or less durable machines and equipment created for industry and consumers. Hand processing of materials dates to the dawn of humanity. Mechanization began with the 18th-century Industrial Revolution, and the first basic machines for forming, shaping, and cutting were invented in the early 19th century, primarily in England. Since then, materials-processing technologies, techniques, and machinery have expanded in variety and quantity. After raw materials are mined from minerals or created from basic chemicals or natural substances, the cycle of manufacturing processes that convert resources into parts and products begins. In most cases, metallic raw materials are synthesized in two stages. Beneficiation is the process of increasing the concentration of the desired metal in the unrefined material. Crushing, roasting, magnetic separation, flotation, and leaching are common beneficiation procedures. Second, further procedures like smelting and alloying are employed to create the metal that will be produced into parts assembled into a finished product. Chemical processes are used to make plastic resins in powder, pellet, putty, or liquid form. Chemical procedures are also used to make synthetic rubber, which is available as slabs, sheeting, crepe, and foam for fabrication into completed parts, just like natural rubber. The methods used to turn raw materials into final products have two fundamental functions: first, they shape the material Inventory and it's Types 28 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes into the required shape, and second, they change or improve the material's qualities. Forming and shaping procedures can be divided into two categories: those performed on a liquid substance and those performed on a solid or plastic material. Most casting and moulding processes involve four major steps: Fig. 2.6: Processes of Casting and Moulding By applying force or pressure to materials in their solid state, they can be shaped into the required/desired shapes. The material to be processed can be rigid and stable, in the shape of a bar, sheet, pellet, or can be soft, in form of plastic, powder or putty like. Solid materials can be changed into desired forms by hot or cold forming processes. Metals in the solid state are processed in two stages: first, large ingots or billets are hot worked into smaller shapes and sizes, usually by rolling, forging, or extrusion; and second, these shapes are processed into final parts and products by one or more than one smaller scale hot or cold forming processes. The material is generally altered after it has been created. A “removal” procedure in materials processing involves removing sections of a piece or body of material to produce a particular shape. Although removal methods can be employed on a variety of materials, they are most used on metals. Mechanical or non-mechanical methods can be used to remove additional material from a work piece. Metal cutting can be done in a variety of ways. Machining is a controlled material-removal technique that cuts a material (typically metal) to a specified final form and size. Cutting equipment, a mechanism of holding and placing the work piece, using lubricants/cutting oil which are basically the components of machining.. Non-cutting removal can be broken down into four steps: 1. Chemical milling removes metal by etching it with chemical solutions; it is most employed on metals, although it can also be used on polymers and glass. Inventory and it's Types 29 Notes Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types 2. Electrochemical machining reverses the principle of metal plating, in which the work piece is eaten away in a controlled manner by the action of the electrical current, rather than being built up by the plating process. 3. High-energy sparks or electrical discharges erode or cut the metal during electrical discharge machining and grinding. 4. Laser machining uses a powerful beam of light from a laser to cut metallic or refractory materials. This category includes both heat-treating (at temperatures above room temperature) and cold treating (at temperatures below room temperature). Thermal treatment is a procedure that involves raising or lowering the temperature of a substance in order to change its properties. Most thermal-treating procedures are based on three-step time-temperature cycles: heating, holding at temperature and cooling. Although some thermal treatments are appropriate to various materials, metals are the most employed. Finally, “finishing” processes can modify the surfaces of materials to protect them from corrosion, oxidation, mechanical wear, or deformation; to provide special surface characteristics such as reflectivity, electrical conductivity or insulation, or bearing properties; or to add decorative effects. There are two types of finishing processes: those that apply a coating to the surface, usually of a different material, and those that modify the material's surface through chemical action, heat, or mechanical force. The methods used to turn raw materials into final products have ..................fundamental functions. (a) 3 (b) 4 (c) 2 (d) 5 4. Non-cutting removal can be broken down into .................. steps. (a) 2 (b) 3 (c) 5 (d) 4 5. Forming and shaping procedures can be divided into .................. categories. (a) 2 (b) 3 (c) 4 (d) 5 2.5 WORK-IN-PROGRESS (WIP) WIP (work-in-progress) is a manufacturing and supply-chain management phrase that refers to partially finished goods that are waiting to be finished. Raw materials, labour, and overhead expenditures for items in various manufacturing process phases are referred to as WIP. WIP is a component of the inventory asset account on the balance sheet. These expenses are then transferred to the finished goods account, and then to the cost of sales account. WIPs are one of a company's balance sheet's components. Fig. 2.7: Work-In-Progress The WIP figure solely reflects the value of products in various stages of production. This does not include the value of raw materials that have not yet been incorporated into a product for sale. Inventory and it's Types 31 Notes Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's TypesThe WIP figure also excludes the value of finished products being held as inventory in anticipation of future sales. The balance in WIP indicates all production expenses incurred for partially produced goods. When combs are made, plastic is used as a raw material in the manufacturing process. Then there's the cost of manpower to run the moulding machines. All costs are added to WIP because the combs are only half-finished. When the combs are finished, the costs are transferred from WIP to finished items, and both accounts are accounted for in the inventory account. When the combs are eventually sold, the costs are transferred from inventory to cost of goods sold (COGS). When inventory has been combined with human labour but has not yet attained final goods status, it is regarded as a WIP. It has been used for some, but not all, of the essential labour. Various accounting procedures can be used to determine WIP and other inventory accounts in different firms. As a result, investors must understand how a company calculates its WIP and other inventory accounts. The WIP of one company may not be similar to that of another. For example, overhead can be allocated based on labour or machine hours. On the balance sheet, WIP is also an asset. It is standard practice to minimize the amount of WIP inventory before reporting is necessary since estimating the percentage of completion for an inventory asset is difficult and time-consuming. 1. Work-in-Progress Calculation Work-in-progress inventory can be calculated in a variety of methods in accounting. Typically, the percentage of total overhead, labour, and material costs incurred by the company is used to compute the number of partially produced products in WIP. A construction company, for example, may bill a client based on the project's progress, such as when it is 25% or 50% complete, and so on. 2. Works-in-Progress vs. Finished Goods The distinction between WIP and finished goods is determined by the inventory's relative completion state, which in this case refers to sale ability. WIP stands for work-in-progress inventory, which began as raw materials and is currently undergoing development or assembly into the finished product. The term “finished goods” refers to the ultimate stage of inventory, when a product has reached a point of completion and is ready for sale to a consumer. The terms “work-in- progress” and “completed items” are relative expressions that pertain to a specific company's inventory accounting. They aren't exact descriptions of real-world materials or things. It's a common misconception that finished goods for one company are classified as finished goods for another. Inventory and it's Types 32 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes Fig. 2.8: Works-in-Progress vs. Finished Goods Sheet plywood, for example, may be regarded a finished commodity by a timber mill since it is ready to sell, but it is considered raw material by an industrial cabinet builder. As a result, the distinction between WIP and finished goods is determined by the state of completion of an inventory in relation to its overall inventory. The terms “work in progress” (WIP) and “completed items” denote the intermediate and end stages of the inventory life cycle, respectively. 3. What is Finished Goods Inventory? Finished products are items that have gone through the production process or have been purchased in a finished state but have not yet been sold to customers. Merchandise refers to items that have been purchased in their finished state. Because these things are expected to be sold in less than a year, the cost of completed goods inventories is considered a short-term asset. The total amount of finished goods inventory on hand at the conclusion of a reporting period is often combined with raw material and work-in-process costs and shown on the balance sheet as a single “Inventory” line item. 4. Finished Goods Definition Inventory extends to finished items and products. They are products that have reached the point in the production process where they may be purchased by the general public. Companies use a formula to calculate the value of finished goods and products in order to produce an inventory ratio that determines the worth of goods for sale. Finished inventory products, as opposed to long-term assets, are typically termed short-term assets because they are intended to be sold in less than a year. 5. Finished Goods Example A variety of factors determines finished goods. Niche Bakers, for example, is a baked goods firm based in Canada. While their completed goods are ready-to-sell Inventory and it's Types 33 Notes Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Typessweets (such as cakes and cookies), the assets that contribute to the finished goods are supplies, cookware, and any labour required for preparation. 6. Finished Goods Inventory Calculation The value of finished goods and basic inventory valuation is calculated by all types of businesses. This calculation can assist business owners in determining the value of their assets to avoid waste and assure profitability. It is an examination of the link between product input and output. This ensures that inventory isn't created at a rate that sales can't keep up with and prevents goods from being sold when it isn't available. The formula is straightforward: Cost of Goods Manufactured – Cost of Goods Sold + Finished Goods Value from the Previous Reporting Period) = Finished Goods Inventory Using the bakery example, if a company manufactures Rs 1000 worth of baked goodies and sells Rs 900, but has Rs 100 worth of sellable baked treats left over from the day before, their finished goods inventory value is Rs200. = (1000 - 900 + 100) = 200 7. Why Finished Goods Inventory Is Important The majority requires an operating budget of successful firms. One of the most important aspects of an operational budget understands your finished goods inventory. It assigns a numerical value to each factor contributing to the end product's manufacturing. You can better calculate the prices at which the product must be sold once you have a numerical value for each aspect of production. When you have a good understanding of costs, you can alter them. For example, if you needed to cut costs, one simple method (using the bakery as an example) could be to reduce food waste. If you are a company producing a product with an expiration date, understanding your finished goods inventory is even more crucial. 8. Using Finished Goods Inventory in Your Business While a simple equation may be used to establish a company's finished products inventory, many businesses find it difficult to appropriately value their finished goods inventory and assets. Even though inventory management ideas are available, inventory management becomes increasingly complex as businesses grow. Many businesses use automated inventory management software to increase Inventory and it's Types 34 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes their production pace, accuracy, and customer service. Inventory management software that is automated can help with things like: z Keeping track of part levels - creating purchase/sales orders, pick tickets, and any other order-related documents z Product location in a warehouse - generating location maps to aid in product discovery in a vast warehouse. z Shipment - allows inventory to flow in and out while also providing shipping information. Manufacturing costs - generating costs, but also organizing and reporting that data. Businesses can benefit from plugin products in addition to inventory management software that facilitates finished goods inventory. When a company understands the elements that support production, it may alter those factors to meet long and short-term commercial objectives while also ensuring overall profitability. 9. Factors influencing the calculation of Finished Goods (i) Cost associate: Assessing the cost of inventory may be a simple process for retailers. Conversely, for manufacturers knowing the cost of finished goods in inventory involves the inclusion of cost from the initial raw materials to finished products ready for sale to customers. The cost of finished goods includes all expense along the way and includes the three main components that go into producing goods — direct labour, direct materials and overhead. In addition, when finished goods are maintained in inventory, a firm will incur carrying costs. (ii) Direct Labour: For retailers, determining the cost of inventory may be a simple task. On the other hand, manufacturers need to know the cost of finished goods in inventory, which includes everything from raw materials to finished goods ready for sale to clients. The cost of finished items comprises all costs incurred along the process and the three basic components of goods production: direct labour, direct materials, and overhead. Furthermore, a company will suffer carrying costs if finished goods are kept in inventory. (iii) Overhead: This section should contain any and all expenses that can be directly or indirectly attributable to the manufacturing of completed items but do not fall into the categories of direct labour or direct materials. This category includes a variety of expenditures ranging from insurance and taxes to depreciation of facilities and equipment, all of which should be allocated pro rata per unit to the cost of finished products. Inventory and it's Types 35 Notes Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types (iv) Carrying Costs: Suppose a big percentage of assets are tied up in inventory. In that case, inventory control may significantly influence total earnings for enterprises that must stock huge amounts of finished goods until sales orders are received. Storage costs, which are charges spent by putting finished goods in inventory, comprise the cost of the space as well as utilities to maintain the space, such as power for air temperature control. In addition, keeping products in stock comes with expenditures for security and insurance to secure the inventory. If purchasers require access to finished items, providing visibility, where applicable, may incur an additional cost. INTEXT QUESTIONS 2.5 1. WIP stands for? (a) work-in-progress (b) work-in-process (c) work-in-plan (d) work-in-procedure 2. .................. products are items that have gone through the production process or have been purchased in a finished state but have not yet been sold to customers. (a) production pace (b) accuracy (c) customer service (d) all of the above 2.6 V ALUE CHAIN A value chain is a business model that outlines the entire process of creating a product or service. The stages involved in moving a product from conception to Inventory and it's Types 36 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes distribution and everything in between – such as obtaining raw materials, manufacturing operations, and marketing activities – make up a value chain for organizations that manufacture things. Value-chain analysis is carried out by examining the procedures involved in each phase of a company's operations. Value-chain analysis is used to improve manufacturing efficiency so that a company may give the most value for the least amount of money. 1. Primary Activities There are five components to primary activities, all of which are necessary for generating value and gaining competitive advantage: z Receiving, warehousing and inventory management are all part of inbound logistics. z Procedures for transforming raw resources into a completed product are included in operations. z Outbound logistics refers to the operations involved in getting a finished product to a customer. z Advertising, promotion, and pricing are all methods used in marketing and sales to increase visibility and target the right clients. z Service comprises customer service, maintenance, repair, refund, and exchange that help keep items in good working order and improve the user experience. 2. How Do Value Chains Work? The value chain framework aids firms in identifying and categorizing their primary and secondary business operations. Organizations can better comprehend these value chain activities, sub-activities, and relationships by analyzing them as a system of interconnected operations. Then, based on the cost, time, and effort required, businesses can review each action or sub-activity individually to see if the output of each activity or sub-activity can be enhanced. A value chain analysis is when a business applies the value chain concept to its activities. Companies must constantly analyze the value they provide to maintain their competitive advantage in the face of increased competition for great prices, exceptional products, and client loyalty. A value chain can assist a corporation in identifying inefficient sections of its business and then implementing ways to optimize its procedures for optimum efficiency and profitability. Inventory and it's Types 37 Notes Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types3. Porter's Value Chain “The value chain concept is based on the process view of organizations, which envisions a manufacturing (or service) organization as a system made up of subsystems, each with its own set of inputs, transformation processes, and outputs.” The acquisition and consumption of resources - money, labour, materials, equipment, buildings, land, administration, and management - are all part of the inputs, transformation processes, and outputs. Costs and earnings are determined by how value chain activities are carried out. In the process of transforming inputs to outputs, most businesses engage in hundreds, if not thousands, of actions. According to Porter (1985), these activities can be classed as primary or support activities that all organizations must conduct in some way.” Fig. 2.9: Porter's Value Chain 4. Examples of Value Chains Here are some examples of tech/e-commerce giant Abcid's primary activities. (i) Inbound logistics. Here are some of the main activities of the tech/e- commerce behemoth Abcid. Logistics on the way in. Products supplied through Abcid's own fulfilment services and data center resources that power Abcid's cloud offerings are the company's key inputs. Abcid can make use of its size as a major company to cut the cost per unit of items it buys from outside vendors. (ii) Operations: This is when Abcid converts its inputs into outputs. Amazon's fundamental offering, its online marketplace, provides a secure venue for both users and sellers to conduct e-commerce transactions. Because Abcid fulfilment and logistics can provide Prime members with two-day shipping, the outcome is a safe, user-friendly consumer experience with significantly shorter shipping periods than competitors at a same price range. Another Inventory and it's Types 38 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes Abcid business unit, AWS, converts inputs into outputs by combining cloud servers, storage, and other data centre resources into a streamlined service for clients' hosting applications and other data. (iii) Outbound logistics: Abcid's outbound logistics includes Abcid fulfillment centres, digital delivery, co-sourcing and outsourcing, and brick-and-mortar stores. (iv) Robotics are used at Abcid's 109 fulfilment facilities to provide quick and cost-effective warehouse labour. Abcid's two-day shipping is a significant advantage over its competitors. (v) Some services, such as Amazon's online marketplace software and Abcid cloud services, may be delivered digitally through co-sourcing and outsourcing arrangements, allowing Abcid to extend beyond the capability of its in-house fulfilment services. (vi) Whole Foods and numerous Abcid retail locations are examples of brick- and-mortar stores. Other Abcid main functions, like service, are aided by these physical retail spaces. Online returns, for example, can be dropped off at select Whole Foods, Kohl's department stores, and UPS facilities. INTEXT QUESTIONS 2.6 1.
