(a) fulfilment GLOSSARY z Overhead: Overhead refers to the ongoing business expenses not directly attributed to creating a product or service z WIP: Work in Progress z Value Chain: the process or activities by which a company adds value to an article, including production, marketing, and the provision of after- sales service. z Finished Goods: Finished goods are inventory items unique to manufacturers. z Automation: the use or introduction of automatic equipment in a manufacturing or other process or facility Inventories and it's Usage 42 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes Manufacturing Companies: Manufacturing institutions use labour and machinery to convert materials into finished goods. For example, after applying labour, a furniture manufacturer converts wood and other materials into finished furniture. 2. Nature of Inventories Trading Companies: Opening and closing inventories are only of one type (i.e., merchandise in trade). Manufacturing Companies: Opening and closing inventories are of three types (i.e., raw- materials, work-in-process, and finished goods). 3. Accounting Data Trading Companies: Given that trading companies do not produce anything, the accumulation of accounting data relevant to production processes is not needed in these organizations. Manufacturing Companies: Accounting procedures provide for the accumulation of accounting data identified with production processes. 4. Determination of Sale Price Trading Companies: The sale price is determined by adding profit to the purchase price. Manufacturing Companies: The sale price is determined by adding profit in per- unit cost. 5. Payroll Trading Companies: Only roll acquaintance is prepared because problems such as manufacturing-related issues do not arise. Manufacturing Companies: Wages and salaries are paid production workers and office employees, therefore separate payrolls are prepared as the cost of the production department is charged to the manufacturing cost, and salaries of office workers are charged to selling and administration costs. Also, a separate payroll is prepared for direct labour and indirect labour. 6. Costs and Expenses Trading Companies: No distinction is made between costs and expenses. Manufacturing Companies : A clear distinction is made between costs and expenses. Inventories and it's Usage 43Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes 3.2 INVENTORIES IN MANUFACTURING COMPANIES Manufacturing inventory, also known as production inventory, refers to all of the resources and materials needed to manufacture a product. Retailers and wholesalers keep only ready-to-sell items, or merchandise inventory, in their inventories. A manufacturing company's inventory, on the other hand, consists of commodities at various stages of production. Inventory is divided into three areas on a manufacturing company's balance sheet: raw materials, work-in-process, and finished goods inventory. Fig. 3.1: Inventories in Manufacturing Companies 3.3 RAW MATERIALS INVENTORY In production inventory management, raw materials inventory includes all of the most basic materials. Produce, individual food items, metal, plastic, stone, chemicals, and everything else that goes into final goods are all examples of this. Take for example a company that makes jellies and jams. Fruit and sugar (for the jam) and glass would be some of the key raw ingredients (for the jars). 3.4 WORK IN PROCESS INVENTORY Work-In-Process inventory, also known as work in progress inventory, refers to inventory that has passed the raw material stage but has not yet reached the completed product stage. The raw materials have been developed, but there are still several boxes to check before the product is ready for sale. This could, for example, be a huge vat of jam that's still simmering and needs to be finished, put into jars, and sealed. It could possibly be jam jars that are still sealed but haven't Inventories and it's Usage 44 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes been labelled. WIP inventory is anything that comes after raw materials but before final goods. Fig. 3.2: Work in Process Inventory 3.5 FINISHED GOODS INVENTORY The end of the line is finished items. They've made it through the entire production process and are ready to depart the nest. A finished good is one that is ready for sale and transportation. To return to the example, this would be jam jars that had been sealed and labelled for shipment. These three forms of inventory are the components that make up manufacturing inventory as a whole. However, another sort of inventory isn't usually included in the basic three categories of manufacturing inventory. That is how inventory is packaged. Fig. 3.3: Finished Goods Inventory 3.6 IMPORTANCE OF MANUFACTURING INVENTORY Manufacturing inventory is critical to the supply chain and directly impacts each company's capacity to satisfy demand. Because manufacturing inventory is Inventories and it's Usage 45Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes necessary for production, low levels can result in an inability to complete orders or heaps of resources that eat into your budget. Worse, these issues are felt throughout the supply chain, causing a bull whip effect that results in financial losses for all the involved organizations. Manufacturing inventory must be meticulously organized to guarantee that the right amount is always accessible. You must also take care of this inventory or risk losing valuable merchandise and hurting client relationships. The best way to do this is to invest in an inventory management system and educate your employees to complete effective inventory control duties. Fig. 3.4: Importance of Manufacturing Inventory 3.7 ACCOUNTING FOR MANUFACTURING INVENTORY Manufacturing inventory is useless if you aren't tracking and accounting for its usage. Here are the most important things to account for: 1. Inventory value. Because industrial inventory is frequently ordered and held in large numbers, it is critical to know how much money you have invested in it. Decide whether you'll use the FIFO or LIFO method of inventory costing, then use that information to calculate the worth of all remaining inventory. This also ensures the accuracy of your balance sheet. 2. Overhead costs. Inventory storage is costly. You must account for every penny spent on your merchandise, from electricity to rent to employee compensation. To get the most accurate figures, try allocating these Inventories and it's Usage 46 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes expenses to each step of the manufacturing and storage process. This will aid in the identification of locations where costs are higher than necessary. 4. Cost of goods sold (COGS). COGS refers to the total cost of all materials used in the production of a product. This figure, along with overhead costs, represents the total cost of running your inventory operations. It's worth noting that atypical expenditures like scrap and waste are included in COGS rather than inventory costs. 5. Inventory levels. If you don't know how much manufacturing inventory you have, you can't measure its worth or use. Maintain a continuous inventory system and make sure that everyone follows the proper processes. This will provide you with the most accurate data from which to act. INTEXT QUESTIONS 3.1 1. What do you mean by Work in Progress? 2. What is the Finished Goods Inventory? 3.8 INVENTORIES IN TRADING BUSINESSES Inventories are the value of stocks of goods held for sale in trading business. The inventory estimates represent the value, at cost, of the items available for sale as of the last day of the reporting period. Valuation methods may differ according to the accounting practices of each company. The estimates given in report are valued on a non-LIFO (last in, first out) basis. Note – LIFO is a method of assessing inventory where the latest items of stock added to the inventory are the first ones taken out. Non-LIFO mean another method, such as FIFO (first in, first out), was used to ascertain the value of the inventory available for sale. Merchandise inventories are shown for warehouses and stores servicing retail businesses. Included are only those warehouses that maintained merchandise supplies mainly intended for distribution within the organization. Retailers and wholesalers typically acquire merchandise that is ready for sale to consumers. Acquisition cost becomes the basis for carrying the inventory until it is put on sale. Inventory refers to the products stocked for future use. All the retail chain has its own warehouse to stock the products to be used when the existing stock replenishes. Inventory management refers to the storage of products to be used at the time of crisis. Retail inventory management is the process of confirming you Inventories and it's Usage 47Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes carry merchandise that consumers want, with neither too much nor too little on hand. By managing inventory, retailers meet consumer demand without running out of stock or holding excess supply. In routine, successful retail inventory management results in lower costs and a clearer understanding of sales patterns. Retail inventory management methods and tools give retailers more information with which to run their businesses, including: Fig. 3.5: Retail inventory management methods INTEXT QUESTIONS 3.2 1. What is LIFO? 2. What is FIFO? 3.9 FACTORS INFLUENCING DEMAND AND SUPPLY OF INVENTORIES Factors Affecting Inventory Management There are several aspects to consider while managing your inventory processes. Both external and internal factors can have an impact on inventory management, therefore it's crucial to be aware of them. Let's look at the key influences on inventory management. Inventories and it's Usage 48 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes (i) Financial Factors Inventory management can be strongly influenced by factors such as the cost of borrowing money to stock enough inventory. In this instance, your finances may fluctuate depending on the economy, so keeping an eye on shifting interest rates to assist plan your expenditures is a good idea. Another element that can influence inventory management is the tax charges involved with stocking inventories. This is especially important when preparing tax returns at the end of the year. Fig. 3.6: Financial Factors Other financial considerations include the costs of warehouse operations and transportation. Changes in these elements may necessitate adjusting your inventory management procedures. Fuel price fluctuations, for example, may force you to reconsider your mobility choices in order to save money. You can either buy your own vehicles or use outside transportation companies, which will modify the way you manage inventories. (ii) Suppliers Suppliers can have a significant impact on inventory management. To manage spending and schedule production, successful businesses require dependable suppliers. An unreliable or unpredictable source can severely harm inventory control. To avoid product shortages or delays in the manufacturing process, it's good to have a reliable backup source. Inventories and it's Usage 49Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes Fig. 3.7: Suppliers (iii) Lead Time The time it takes for an item to arrive from the time it is ordered is known as lead time. Modifications in these parameters can necessitate changes in inventory management. Lead time varies greatly due to product type and production processes involved. Though outsourcing manufacturing operations result in reduced production costs, it might lead to extended waiting time. However, producing the same goods locally may cost more but would take less time, therefore your stock levels may need to be adjusted accordingly. Fig. 3.8: Lead Time (iv) Product Type Inventory control must consider the various sorts of products on hand. Some products, for example, are perishable and hence have a shorter shelf life than Inventories and it's Usage 50 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes others. Inventory must be handled carefully in this situation to guarantee that these items are cycled according to expiration dates. Fig. 3.9: Product Type (v) Management Finally, you and any co-owners are responsible for controlling your company's inventory. While you may have numerous managers overseeing inventory procedures, they are unlikely to have the same financial stake in the company as you. (vi) External Factors Multiple external factors can have an impact on inventory control. Economic downturns, for example, may occur, and you will often have very little control over this. In order to avoid stock outs or surplus inventory, it is necessary to assess the economy. Other considerations include the health of the real estate markets and level of local competition among others. Because these factors are mainly out of your control, it's a good idea to monitor the external climate on a frequent basis to keep prepared. (vii) Market Demand For successful inventory management, it is critical to understand market demand as everything is dependent on supply and demand. It is critical to provide products on schedule in order to satisfy the customers. A firm manufactures goods/products in response to customer demands, which varies from time to time. For example, a charger is required to charge a mobile phone; and without one, the mobile phone Inventories and it's Usage 51Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes will become useless. Therefore it is critical to have adequate chargers for phones meaning adequate supply to match the demand. Thus, it is imperative that one must keep track of demand and supply and plan their inventory accordingly. (viii) Inventory Theft Theft of inventory is one of the most widespread problems in the world. It's also one of the factors that have an impact on inventory. According to the Tech jury, employee theft accounts for 28% of retail inventory losses in the United States. For example, you believe merchandise is in stock, but you do not locate inventory as expected when you check your warehouse. Inventory theft also has a negative impact on business. (ix) Lead Time Everything must be provided swiftly in today's competitive environment, which is why supply chains are growing increasingly complex. Lead time is extremely important for firms that import inventory from another country or region via plane or ship. When inventory is shipped from this far away, it takes time to arrive, hence this circumstance has an influence on inventories. As a result, a plan must be devised that considers all of these elements in order for inventory management remain unaffected. (x) Forecast Quality and Quantity The accuracy of the record is critical for forecasting the quality and quantity. Forecasting inventories in small and medium-sized businesses is straightforward. However, in large enterprises, the procedure is complicated, especially when a large number of inventory enter and exit. As previously stated, accuracy is critical. By evaluating the prior order frequency and comprehending the pattern, you may determine what your client wants. Furthermore, quality impacts corporate operations, and inaccuracy can lead to errors. As a result, inventory control might fall short of expectations. (xi) Vendors and Manufacturers Vendor collaboration is critical, as vendors are the most persuasive variable influencing inventory management. Monitoring and maintaining the supply chain can be difficult for businesses, especially if they can't rely on vendors to meet deadlines and provide high-quality goods. Diversification is used in the finance business to reduce risk. Investing all of your money in a single source might be dangerous. Similarly, you should not rely on a single vendor for inventory Inventories and it's Usage 52 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes management. If something goes wrong with the vendor's business, your business will also suffer, therefore it is advisable to have multiple vendors. Other advantages to picking multiple providers, also include better negotiations for prices, low delivery time, increased service quality and additional items among others. INTEXT QUESTIONS 3.3 1. List the factors influencing demand and supply of inventories? 2. What is Lead Time? 3.10 INFORMATION ABOUT DEMAND AND SUPPLY 1. Supply and Demand The most fundamental economic notions are supply and demand. The essential concept of supply and demand equilibrium is integrated into your daily actions whether you are an academic, farmer, pharmaceutical producer, or simply a customer. The more difficult components of economics can only be comprehended after comprehending the fundamentals of these models. 2. Demand Although most explanations begin with the concept of supply, comprehending demand is more obvious for many people and thus aids subsequent explanations. From the consumer's perspective, the graph above demonstrates the most basic relationship between the price of a commodity and its demand. This is one of the most significant distinctions between the supply and demand curves. Fig. 3.10: Demand Inventories and it's Usage 53Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes Demand graphs are produced from the perspective of the consumer, whereas supply graphs are drawn from the perspective of the supplier. Except in a few unusual circumstances, as the price of a good rises, so does the demand for it. Let's pretend the product in question is a television set for the sake of this debate. If televisions are sold for Rs.5 each, a big number of people will buy them frequently. Most people would buy more televisions than they require, placing one in each room and possibly storing others in the basement. Because almost everyone can afford a television, demand for these products will continue to be high. If a television set costs Rs.50,000, however, it will be an uncommon consumer item because only the rich can buy it. While most people still want to buy TVs, demand for them at that price would be exceedingly low. Of course, none of the preceding cases occur in a vacuum. A simple demand model assumes a number of conditions. First, there is no product differentiation—every consumer receives the same type of product at the same price. Second, in this confined scenario, the thing in question is a basic want rather than a basic human need like food (although having a TV provides a definite level of utility, it is not an absolute requirement). Third, there is no substitute for the good, and customers anticipate pricing to be stable in the future. 3. Supply The supply curve functions similarly, but it considers the relationship between the price and available supply of an item from the producer's perspective rather than the consumer. When a product's price rises, producers are eager to produce more of it in order to make more money. Similarly, declining prices reduce output since producers may not be able to recoup their input costs when selling the finished product. Returning to the television set as an example, if the input costs to make a TV are fixed at Rs.50 plus variable labour expenses, production would be severely unprofitable once the TV's selling price falls below Rs.50. When prices are higher, however, producers are driven to raise their output in order to maximize their profits. If the price of a television set is Rs.1,000, for example, producers can concentrate on creating television sets in addition to other potential businesses. Keeping all other variables constant, but raising the TV's selling price to Rs.50,000 would benefit the producers and create an incentive to build more televisions. The supply curve is forced to be upward sloping by the habit of seeking maximum profits. The theory's basic assumption is that the producer will act as a price taker. Rather of dictating product prices, the market determines this input, and providers are only faced with the issue of how much to produce given the market price. In Inventories and it's Usage 54 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes monopolistic marketplaces, ideal situations are not always the case, like the demand curve. Fig. 3.11: Supply INTEXT QUESTIONS 3.4 1. Define Supply. 2. Define Demand. 3.11 ECONOMIC ORDER QUANTITY (EOQ) The best order quantity for a corporation to purchase to reduce inventory costs such as holding charges, shortage costs, and order fees is the economic order quantity (EOQ). Did you Know Ford W. Harris created this production-scheduling concept in 1913, and it has been modified over time. Demand, ordering, and holding costs are all assumed to be constant in the formula. Basic EOQ Assumptions 1. Demand is constant, known, and independent. 2. The lead time: the time between the placement of the order and the receipt of the order – is known and constant. 3. Receipt of inventory is complete and instantaneous. Inventories and it's Usage 55Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes 4. Quantity discounts are not possible. 5. The only variable costs are the cost of placing an order, ordering cost, and the cost of storing or holding inventory over time, carrying, or holding, cost. 6. Stockouts can be totally avoided Formula for Calculating Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Formula for EOQ EOQ = ( )square root of 2 D S H o×× 3.12 WHAT THE ECONOMIC ORDER QUANTITY CAN TELL YOU The EOQ formula aims to determine the best number of product units to order. A company's costs for purchasing, transporting, and storing units can be reduced if this goal is met. The EOQ formula can be tweaked to establish different production levels or order intervals, and companies with complex supply chains and high variable costs employ a computer algorithm to calculate EOQ. The EOQ is a crucial cash flow tool. The method can assist a business manage the quantity of cash it has in its inventory. Inventory is, aside from human resources, the most valuable asset for many firms, and these enterprises must keep enough inventory on hand to meet client demands. If EOQ can help reduce inventory levels, the money saved can be put toward another business project or investment. The EOQ formula establishes the inventory reorder point of a corporation. When inventory levels drop below a specific threshold, the EOQ formula, when applied to business operations, signals the need to place a new order for more units. The business avoids running out of inventory and can continue to fill customer orders by setting a reorder point. When a corporation runs out of inventory, it incurs a shortfall cost, which is revenue lost due to a lack of inventory to fulfil an order. A scarcity of inventory could result in the company losing a customer or the client ordering less in the future. 3.13 EXAMPLE OF HOW TO USE EOQ The EOQ considers the timing of reordering, the cost of placing an order, and the cost of storing products. The ordering expenses are higher and there is a need for more storage space if a corporation is frequently placing minor purchases to maintain a specified inventory level. Consider the case of a retail apparel store that carries a line of men's jeans and sells 1,000 pairs every year. A pair of jeans costs the corporation Rs.5 per year to have in inventory, and placing an order costs Rs.2. Inventories and it's Usage 56 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes The square root of (2 × 1,000 pairs x Rs.2 order cost) / (Rs.5 holding cost), or 28.3 with rounding, is the EOQ formula. To cut expenses and match consumer demand, the ideal order size is slightly more than 28 pairs of jeans. The reorder point is determined by a more complicated part of the EOQ calculation. INTEXT QUESTIONS 3.5 1. What is Economic Order Quantity? 2. What is the formula used for calculating Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)? 3.14 MANAGING INVENTORY WHEN DEMAND IS KNOWN Neither too low nor too high levels of inventory are good for the health of any company. At the same time, there is no cut-and-dried formula to predict or estimate demand as there is no thumb rule to forecast demand, particularly in the extremely dynamic marketplace. Also worldwide, companies are constantly under huge pressure to cut their total logistics costs, particularly inventory costs. Usually, corporate businesses have enjoyed a sellers' market where demand always excels supply. Companies' environmental scenario was more stable as well as generally predictable. Under a market scenario of certainty, companies usually emphasize primarily on two issues which needs appropriate attention and analysis for the proper management of inventory level.. 1. Order quantity. How much to order of each material with either outside suppliers or the production department within the company. It is also called Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) or Lot Sizes. 2. Order points. When to place the orders. It is also known as Reorder Point (RP). INTEXT QUESTIONS 3.6 1. What are basic assumptions of EOQ? 2. What is Order Quantity and Order Point? 3.15 MANAGING INVENTORY WHEN DEMAND IS UNCERTAIN FMCGs have a large client demand and manufactured items depreciate quickly due to a short product life shelf; meaning, goods that are usually transacted and Inventories and it's Usage 57Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes consumed in a short period of time. Inventory management is critical to the success of industrial companies. Raw materials, work-in-progress, consumables, and completed items for sale can all be found in inventories. For an organization's success in this competitive market is dependent on their inventory management system. Inventory management aims to strike a balance between lowering total costs and increasing customer satisfaction. These roles are not simple to fill because of the effects and sporadic occurrences on demand uncertainty especially in the developing countries. Inventory management is all about low inventory levels, low pricing, and great customer satisfaction; it's about profitable value-added procedures and processes. Inventory is a significant portion of an organization's investment, hence its management would determine a company's success or failure. Incompetency in managing inventory could lead to drop in sales and might lead to business failures. Inventory management has grown greatly in recent years, and the deployment of professional organization processes on inventory management is critical. The issue is that inventory managers sometimes fail to satisfy demand, which is critical for FMCG manufacturers. Reasons for poor inventory management, includes poor implementation of inventory systems and processes, lack of demand along with non-training/lack of training of personnel. 3.16 INVENTORY MODEL Answering the main questions of how much to order and when to order are central to all inventory models. Inventory serves numerous key tasks in an organization, including reducing total expenses. Economic order quantity (EOQ) is an inventory model that is used for purchase orders with a set order quantity and helps to reduce total inventory costs. Inventory costs according to business types, and are also extremely expensive. There are three categories of inventory expenses including carrying costs, ordering costs, and shortfall costs. In production planning and control, uncertainty poses significant obstacles. Models for inventory systems consider whether demand and supply are certain or uncertain. However, demand fluctuates constantly due to changes in orders, suppliers' erratic capacity, and unpredictable events such as weather, equipment malfunctions, and human errors. When we talk about uncertainty, we need safety stock to address the chance of stock- outs caused by unpredictable demand. When demand is unknown, the application of designed EOQ on regular demand has the greatest impact on stock-out. To avoid a stock-out situation, increase the reorder point by keeping excess stock on hand as safety stock. The logistician's expression in safety stock terms is the spare stock kept to cover the possibility of stock-outs caused by supply or demand variations. Safety stock, according to websites, is extra planned on-hand inventory that protects against stock-outs. Inventory control and demand uncertainty exist. Inventories and it's Usage 58 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes There is a strong positive relationship between these variables, so the higher the demand uncertainty, the more difficult and challenging it is to keep stock in an organization. Effective demand and forecasting methodologies should be considered by the organization. To meet consumers' expectations, FMCG companies should consider the different types of inventories and how they should be maintained. Understanding the calculations of safety stock is critical to any organization's performance and growth in order to avoid stock-outs. When inventory models are not computed effectively, the organization may end up with too much inventory, tying up its investments, or too little inventory, causing shortages. Proper inventory management avoids any company from having low-quality products, disappointed clients, and loss of money. When inventory kinds are understood and used in an organization, there will be little uncertainty in meeting client demand. Organizations should acknowledge the relationship between inventory management and uncertain demand to keep the inventory at its optimal level, hence fulfilling demand. INTEXT QUESTIONS 3.7 1. LEARNING OUTCOMES After completing this lesson the Learner z defines the term warehousing and its importance in Inventory Management: z identifies the different Inventory Management tools; z explains the concept and usage of ABC analysis of inventory; z applies the Just-in-Time concepts in inventory tools; 4.1 INTRODUCTION TO WAREHOUSING The warehousing operation is an important part of the overall supply chain process because it establishes a clear distinction between supply and demand in any organisation. The process entails storing resources and items throughout the supply chain, from suppliers to end users. The goal of the operation is to ensure that resources and products are stored in a proper condition and are always ready for transportation to the their next step in the supply chain. 4 SORTING INVENTORY AND ZONE ALLOCATION Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 62 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes Fig. 4.1: Warehousing The physical character of the industry has traditionally emphasised the labour- intensive nature of the operation, necessitating a high level of man-management and control. However, because of substantial advancements in technical assistance in both physical and administrative sides of the operation, it is becoming increasingly “system” driven. This progress, combined with a more proactive rather than reactive approach, has highlighted the need for better planning and operational management. Warehousing, like other parts of the supply chain, is concerned with the two primary factors of cost and service by: - (i) Keeping the total expense of the business to a minimum. (ii) Providing the degree of service that is desired. The environment in which warehousing can operate ranges from a single central location that serves as a hub for receiving, storing, and preparing goods for delivery to the entire market with a complex network of central, regional, and local facilities. 4.2 CONSTITUENT ELEMENTS To satisfy the above definition and meet the basic goals related to cost and service, the management of the warehouse operation needs to consider the three major constituent elements of space, labour and equipment as shown in the figure. These factors will reflect the total cost of the warehouse operation, through measuring the utility and performance of each individual part along with the trade- offs made between them to arrive at the best possible solution. The effective and efficient methods and procedures utilised throughout the warehouse operation will define the degree of service delivered to clients. Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 63Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes Fig. 4.2: Constituent Elements In planning the warehouse operation, the objectives can be set to determine the 'best' combination of maximising storage space and minimising handling operations. For achieving these goals several options will be available, each with a different operating profile, cost, and service output. Therefore an iterative method will be used to find the “optimal” answer to achieve the set goals. After the planned base has been created, the warehouse operating cycle is formed in order to carry out, monitor, review, amend, and adjust to suit the short- and medium-term requirements. This process, like other planning cycles, is ongoing and constant, and should not be thought of as a once-a-year effort. A big warehouse used by a company might be seen as failure of proper supply chain management, however there are several reasons companies might need big warehouses, which are discussed next in the section. 4.3 REQUIREMENT OF WAREHOUSE The main reasons for requiring a warehouse are: 1. Stockpiling – The warehouse acts as a buffer between supply and demand, a function which becomes especially important when seasonality is involved. 2. Production – Warehousing is needed for the storage of raw materials, components, sub-assemblies, work in progress and finished goods, to achieve economies in the production process. 3. Product Mixing – To provide a facility for combining several products into a new one through a packaging operation. Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 64 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes 4. Consolidation – The warehouse acts as a consolidation point to 'pull' product from suppliers into one location for sorting after which it is provided to the end customer. 5. Distribution – The warehouse network allows products to be 'pushed' from the manufacturer through the network to the end user. 6. Customer Service – The warehouse is helpful in aiding desired service level to the end user. 4.4 SIGNIFICANCE OF WAREHOUSE MANAGEMENT Warehouse operations are primarily cost-cutting rather than value-adding activity. The business are not creating value if they are holding products or materials and not doing anything with them or pushing them closer to the business end consumer. The cost of keeping materials/goods in a warehouse includes the cost of running and maintaining the warehouse as well as the initial construction and equipment costs. Fig. 4.3: Significance of warehouse management As a result, the operation must be carried out in the most cost-effective manner feasible, with warehouse activities delivering value wherever possible. According to an analysis, warehouse expenditures account for almost 30% of total logistics costs. This will obviously differ depending on the industry sector. Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 65Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes The type of product is also a determining factor is choosing a warehouse — for example, the amount of space each item requires and its qualities and handling, stack-ability, and stock-holding requirements. Goods in various forms must be stored at various points in the supply chain process, but they must transit through the warehouse as rapidly as possible to avoid excessive cost. The warehouse's lathe business and management will significantly impact the speed with which goods can be moved through the warehouse. When products are in a warehouse, it is critical that the business use the operation to add value wherever possible, and maintain proper pace to help in keeping costs low, and reducing non-value-added operations. Most businesses that manufacture, import, export, or transport commodities require warehousing. A warehouse allows the business to keep better track of the business inventory and guarantees that consumers receive their orders on time, resulting in more revenue. 1. Better Inventory Management Around 8% of small enterprises do not keep track of their inventory. About a quarter of businesses have no inventory at all. This frequently results in late delivery, order processing delays, and a negative customer experience. Warehouses keep the business products/goods in one area, making it easier to maintain and manage the business inventory. Therefore it is advisable for businesses to invest in a warehouse as it helps them store, pack and distribute products more efficiently. 2. More Efficient Packing and Processing Most warehouses have the necessary equipment and supplies to store, transfer, package, and handle customer orders. To name a few, there are pallet racks, loading docks, and packing materials. This way, the business will have everything in one spot, saving both time and money. Businesses can pack and grade their items according to legal regulations and client expectations in a warehouse. The cost of logistics is lowered, while flexibility is increased. This type of facility can be an appropriate distribution site because it eliminates the need to schedule pickup and hire fulfilment staff. 3. Ensure Price Stabilisation Customers' income, government policies, employment rates, climatic conditions, and other actors all influence demand for goods and services time to time. When demand is more, a warehouse allows the business to keep business their products for a longer period of time. This contributes to price stability and lowers revenue Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 66 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes losses. Let's pretend a business company makes and sells sports equipment. If the business sell ski gear, instead of selling them at lower cost after cold/snow season, the business can store business their products at a warehouse, which would help them keep stable stocks and maximize profits. business 4. Improved Risk Management Warehousing prevents perishable products from price swings while also ensuring their safety. The business can rent a warehouse with refrigerators, freezers, and temperature control depending on the business demand and type of products. Plants, artwork, candles, food, and pharmaceuticals are just a few of the items that need to be kept cold. A warehouse that provides these services will keep help the business maintain their products and also extends their shelf life, which would lead to profitability. Furthermore, warehoused goods are usually covered by insurance. This implies that in the event of damage, fire, or theft, the business have a better chance of receiving compensation from an insurance company. INTEXT QUESTIONS 4.1 1. What are the elements of warehouse? 2. List the importance of warehouse. 4.5 SORTING AND STORING OF INVENTORIES IN WAREHOUSES 1. Sorting Sorting is a way of categorizing a warehoused object by its qualities and designating an appropriate storage location based on those features and keeping meticulous records. It's a simple notion, but it doesn't always work in practice, and when this crucial component of warehouse management is overlooked, all sorts of issues can arise. When things aren't properly classified, they can vanish from a system, and finding them can be like hunting for a needle in a haystack. If something happens once, it is quite likely to happen again. Ensuring that items are properly organized when they arrive, both in the physical area and in the records the business retains, is a great time and money saver. 2. Storage and Automatic Batch Forming With sorting in place, as items arrive, they are assigned batches and then filed in the appropriate area. Everyone has probably been in a warehouse where objects Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 67Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes are stored wherever there is space, and while this may be required at times, if it is minimized, the time it takes to pick the items can be considerably reduced. In a digital sense, being properly sorted and filed also implies that something may be appropriately identified by its dimensions, the material it is composed of, or whatever criteria the business require. If a batch cannot be kept at the designated location, it will not be lost in the system and separated from the main batch. Sorting allows the business to monitor and identify the location of an object in any situation. 3. Ideal flow Right from the Start It comes to reason that if the business warehouse is highly managed, the business entire supply chain will work much more smoothly. If the business start with a mess, it will spread down the line and have bad consequences all the way from the warehouse to the client. When products are difficult to locate, it is impossible to transmit them on time, and they do not arrive on time. It's the kind of issue that, if not addressed, may quickly escalate. Materials and items are continually arriving, and orders are being placed. Any slowdown in that process will inevitably have an impact on the remainder of the business. Getting the appropriate flow going from the beginning will show in the final output. Sorting is quite beneficial, When it comes to optimizing the business warehouse and making it the most efficient place it can be, sorting is a critical component of the business success. Working with a company that can assist the business in developing a sorting system that works well for the business can make all the difference. 4.6 STORAGE The warehouse is usually an important part of getting products into customers' hands and keeping them satisfied. Without careful planning, the business run the danger of delaying client deliveries and creating an inefficient staffing structure that drives up labour expenses. Inventory management is divided into two categories: physical and digital. Develop a physically and digitally efficient system, allowing the business to streamline processes and increase revenue. 1. Analyze the Goods Demand Examine which of the business inventory items sell the most frequently. These products must be readily available. The business can separate the business warehouse from front to back, categorizing products depending on volume and frequency, by looking at the business inventory from the angle of sales volume. Keeping popular items near the delivery area reduces the time and energy required for personnel to obtain the items and for consumers to leave with them. Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 68 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes 2. Everything should be Mapped and Labelled. Consider the lathe business of a large-scale retailer: There are sections dedicated to the kitchen, the garden, sporting items, children, and so on. A huge hanging placard from the top shelf usually designates each section. Each aisle is assigned a number, and portions may be divided further. The children's area, for example, could be subdivided into nursing, strollers, car seats, and safety goods. Use this as a guide to map the business own warehouse. After the business have items placed in the right area, label them. Labeling includes a physical label and a SKU number that corresponds to the business digital inventory management program. Fig. 4.4: Everything should be labelled and mapped Source: https://www.asgservices.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/04/Warehouse-Labels- 1024x512.jpg 3. Inventory Control Programs Bar code readers scan products in inventory management solutions like Inventory Control. When a customer purchases a product, the programme records the price and modifies the inventory amount, making frequent warehouse inventory counts for quality-control purposes considerably easier. These apps create reports that help the business order inventory more efficiently from vendors. Inventory management systems not only help warehouse workers save time, but they also help them assess whether a product is in stock. 4. System of Maintenance and Cleaning Upkeep is critical to keeping order in any facility. Items are removed from shelves during the course of business. Set up a system to restock shelves with out-of-place Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 69Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes items and execute quality-control checks on a daily, weekly, or monthly basis. After use, equipment and tools should be returned to their proper storage location. Aisles should be kept clean and clutter-free. All of this helps to eliminate the turmoil that occurs when things appear in inventory reports but not on the shelves. Train warehouse staff to do routine maintenance and cleaning. INTEXT QUESTIONS 4.2 1. What is sorting? 2. What is labelling? 4.7 INVENTORY MANAGEMENT TECHNIQUES/TOOLS When a business considers that some companies may need to track hundreds of thousands of stock items, it's no surprise that inventory management software is deemed very important at that point in time. Inventory management is a major concern for many firms, particularly those in manufacturing and wholesale. The most likely aspect of the firm to be computerized is inventory records, and every ERP and CRM system is based around an inventory management feature. A 'perpetual inventory record' is maintained in basic inventory management. Simply put, the system collects inventory movement reports (transactions) and keeps track of the quantity on hand in real time. Of course, inventory management software does a lot more than that. Here are some of the tools that can and should be integrated with inventory management to give the business the control and visibility the business need to support efficient operations. 1. Data collection with Barcodes The perpetual inventory system relies heavily on accurate and timely reporting. Delays, inaccuracies, missing transactions, and an unreasonable strain on the workforce to collect and enter data can all be problems with manual reporting. Automated data gathering, most commonly via barcode scans, alleviates most of the reporting load while vastly enhancing transaction accuracy and timeliness. 2. Counting in Cycles to Improve Accuracy Inventory accuracy is critical for effective inventory planning and control. Unfortunately, most inventory monitoring systems are wildly incorrect without an active and disciplined cycle counting strategy in place. Cycle counting is a regular programme of counting selected things that replaces the tedious and error-prone Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 70 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes periodic (typically annual) physical inventory count with counting more important items more often than less important ones. Cycle counting's beauty is that it gives a structure for finding and eliminating the cause of errors in order to improve accuracy over time. 3. Prioritization using ABC analysis Cycle counting is commonly set up using ABC analysis to identify the more critical and less important things described above. The most frequent ABC analysis (also known as Pareto analysis) method is to rank all inventory items on an annual basis according to their overall value (annual “use” or movement times unit cost). The top 20% of items typically account for 80% of the annual value passing through the warehouse (the 80/20 rule). The following 30 percent (B goods) account for 15% of the value, while the last 50% (C items) account for 5% of the annual value. ABC classifications should drive item location in the warehouse, lot sizing and safety stock regulations, and other management factors, in addition to cycle counting frequency, to focus the business attention on the products that matter, where the business can get the best return on the business investment. 4. Planning and Execution that is Coordinated ERP's main goal is to increase customer service (meet demand) while also optimizing resources, such as inventories (and reducing costs). While the inventory system offers vital information to ERP's planning and execution systems, these functions reciprocate by managing inventory consumption and replenishment to reduce shortages and lower overall inventory investment. Fig. 4.5: Inventory Management Techniques Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 71Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes 5. Traceability and Tracking of Lots Although not every organization requires a serial number or lot tracking traceability, more and more are doing so in response to the growing risk of product recalls. Engineering, development, product service, spare part provisioning, and other organisation sections can benefit greatly from life-cycle information, genealogy, configuration history, and product performance data. Financial control and basic business management operations necessitate inventory tracking. However, inventory management and data can be a useful tool in the pursuit for better performance, better customer service, cost control, product creation, and overall corporate success. Integrated inventory management systems, a critical part of any ERP, provide the basic tools and significant additional capabilities to make ERP of increasing utility, and catered to a bigger business's needs. INTEXT QUESTIONS 4.3 1. What is cycle counting? 2. What is ERP? 4.8 MATERIALS REQUIREMENT PLANNING Material requirements planning (MRP) is a computer-based inventory management solution that helps firms increase their productivity. Material requirements planning systems are used by businesses to predict raw material amounts and schedule deliveries. Fig. 4.6: Materials Requirement Planning Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 72 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes 4.9 HOW DOES MRP (MATERIAL REQUIREMENTS PLANNING) WORK? MRP is intended to respond to three questions: MRP starts with a finished goods production plan, which is translated into a list of needs for the subassemblies, component components, and raw materials required to manufacture the final product on time. In other words, it's a system for determining the materials and equipment required to make a specific product. MRP assists manufacturers in determining inventory needs while balancing supply and demand. This technology parses raw data, such as bills of lading and the shelf life of stored commodities, to offer managers with useful information about their labour and supply needs, which can help organizations improve their production efficiency. Fig. 4.7: Material Requirements Planning 4.10 STEPS OF MATERIAL REQUIREMENTS PROCESS Fig. 4.8: Material Requirements Process 1. Demand estimation and the supplies needed to meet it: The first stage in the MRP process is identifying client demand and the requirements to meet it. MRP breaks down demand into particular raw materials and components using the bill of materials, which is basically a list of raw materials, assemblies, and components required to make an end product. Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 73Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes 2. Compare demand to inventory and allocate resources accordingly: This phase entails comparing demand to what the business already have on hand. The MRP then allocates resources appropriately. In other words, the MRP assigns inventory to the precise regions where it is required. 3. Production planning.: S imply calculate the amount of time and manpower required to finish the manufacturing process with the required timeline. 4. Observe the procedure.: The process's final stage is merely to keep an eye on it for any problems. In order to achieve set deadlines, the MRP may immediately warn management to any delays and even suggest contingency solutions. 4.11 MATERIAL REQUIREMENTS PLANNING (MRP) IN MANUFACTURING A bill of materials (BOM) – an exhaustive inventory of raw materials, components, and assemblies necessary to create, manufacture, or repair a product or service— is an important input for material requirements planning. The relationship between the end product (independent demand) and the components is defined by the bill of materials (BOM) (dependent demand). Dependent demand relates to components, while independent demand comes from outside the plant or manufacturing system. Companies must proactively manage the types and quantities of resources they buy, plan which items to make and in what quantities, and ensure that they can meet current and future client demand – all while keeping costs reasonable. MRP assists businesses in maintaining low inventory levels. The corporation will lose money if it makes a faulty judgement in any part of the production cycle. Manufacturers can better synchronize their production with rising and falling demand by maintaining proper inventory levels. 4.12 MRP SYSTEMS: BACKGROUND Material requirements planning was one of the first integrated information technology (IT) systems, which attempted to boost business productivity by utilizing computers and software. The earliest MRP inventory management systems appeared in the 1940s and 1950s. They converted information from a bill of materials for a specific completed product into a manufacturing and buying plan using mainframe computers. MRP systems soon evolved to include information feedback loops, allowing production managers to alter and update system inputs as needed. Manufacturing resources planning (MRP II), the next version of MRP, included features of marketing, finance, accounting, engineering, and human resources in the planning process. Operation resource planning, which employs Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 74 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes computer technology to connect the various functional areas throughout a complete commercial enterprise, is a related idea that extends on MRP. As data analysis and technology advanced, more complete systems to connect MRP with other areas of the production process were developed. 4.13 ABC ANALYSIS OF INVENTORY What is ABC analysis in Inventory Management? ABC analysis is a strategy for inventory management that assesses the value of inventory items based on their importance to the company. ABC ranks products based on demand, cost, and risk data, and inventory managers classify items according to those criteria. This assists business leaders in determining which items or services are most important to their company's financial success. “Class A” products are the most important stock-keeping units (SKUs) in terms of either sales volume or profitability, while “Class B” items are the next most important and “Class C” things are the least important. Some businesses may opt for a classification system that includes more than just three categories (A-F, for example). Fig. 4.9: ABC Analysis of Inventory ABC analysis in cost accounting, also known as activity-based costing, is similar to but not identical to ABC analysis in inventory management. In manufacturing, activity-based costing is used by accountants to assign indirect or overhead expenses to products and services, such as utilities and salaries. 4.14 ABC INVENTORY CLASSIFICATION The ability to classify inventory by importance is an important part of inventory management. The relevance of each item in the supply chain varies. Some, such as specialized surgical equipment, are extremely critical. Others, like as latex gloves at a hospital, are less critical. The first step in inventory management is to classify Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 75Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes inventory according to its importance in order to appropriately manage it. ABC categorization is the tool for this. We can give priority to significant inventory items and handle them with care by classifying inventory by importance. It also keeps us from spending valuable resources on things that aren't as important. Pareto's Law argues that a tiny fraction of items account for a high percentage of total value. This value could be sales, profits, or any other metric. Approximately 10% to 20% of inventory items contribute for 70% to 80% of total inventory value. These items are designated as A inventory items because they are extremely valuable. Moderate value items make up about 30% of inventories and account for about 35% of the total. They are referred to as B items. Finally, almost half of the items only account for about 10% of the entire inventory value. These are called C items, and they are the least important. ABC analysis is demonstrated in Figure. Fig. 4.10: ABC Inventory Classification The following are the stages to performing an ABC analysis: z Calculate each item's annual consumption or sales. z Calculate the proportion of overall usage or sales for each item. z Sort the elements by percentage from highest to lowest. z Sort the items into categories. Do not push groups to suit the preset percentages after sorting the items from highest to lowest z. These are rough estimations. Natural breaking points, on the other hand, are more likely to occur. These are the classifications that should be utilized because the data will organically categorize themselves. For setting order policies, ABC analysis is critical. For A items, the most complex inventory systems should be employed. Many managers personally supervise these. C products, on the other hand, are usually left to automated ordering systems because they do not justify the expense of administrative intervention. Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 76 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes INTEXT QUESTIONS 4.4 1. What is Category A? 2. What is Category B? 4.15 APPLICATION OF JUST-IN-TIME FOR MATERIALS MANAGEMENT JIT is a type of inventory management that entails working closely with suppliers to ensure that raw supplies arrive on time, but no earlier. The idea is to keep as little inventory on hand as possible to meet demand. 4.16 HOW DOES JUST-IN-TIME INVENTORY MANAGEMENT WORK? JIT inventory management ensures that stock arrives just when it is required for production or to fulfil consumer demand. The goal is to eliminate waste and boost operational efficiency. JIT necessitates long-term partnerships with dependable suppliers because the primary goal is frequently quality rather than the lowest price. A lean management process is what JIT stands for. JIT connects all elements of a manufacturing or service system, notably humans. They share information and are mutually reliant on achieving positive results. Kaizen, a Japanese phrase that means “improvement,” is the source of this approach. The corporate strategy, which originated in Japan, aims to continuously improve operations by involving all employees, from assembly line workers to the CEO, Like JIT, the goal is to reduce waste and improve quality. 4.17 STEPS IN CYCLE OF CONTINUOUS IMPROVEMENT FOR JIT INVENTORY Fig. 4.11: JIT Inventory 1. Design: Product design, process design, people, and manufacturing planning are all reviewed as part of the JIT process. Then strategies are made to minimize disturbance, save waste, and create a flexible system. Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 77Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes 2. Manage: A Total Quality Management (TQM) evaluation ensures that the process is constantly improving. A management review establishes and measures statistical quality control, stabilizes schedules, and verifies load and capacity schedules and levels. 3. Pull: Using signaling systems such as Kanban, educate the team on production and withdrawal processes. Reduce lot sizes by reviewing lot size policies. 4. Establish: Vendor relationships is critical to JIT's success. Examine the vendor lists. Choose preferred suppliers, negotiate contracts, and talk about lead times, delivery expectations, and usage metrics and KPIs. Learn how to utilize them in the business supply chain. 5. Fine-tune: Reduce inventory movements by determining inventory needs, policies, and controls. 6. Build: Educate the business team on the skills and capabilities they'll need to finish their work by holding team education and empowerment sessions. 7. Refine: By refining, standardizing, and assessing the entire process, the business can reduce the number of parts and stages in manufacturing. 8. Review: Define and execute quality measurements and measures and conduct a root cause analysis of any issues. To enhance every component of JIT, emphasize improvements and track trends. 4.18 THE PURPOSE OF JIT When a corporation orders inventory on an as-needed basis, it does not keep any safety stock and maintains consistently low inventory levels. This method aids businesses in lowering inventory carrying costs, improving efficiency, and reducing waste. JIT demands manufacturers to estimate demand for their products with extreme precision. Just-in-time inventory management is a good way to save money on inventory, but it can also result in stockouts. JIT aims to increase a company's return on investment by lowering non-essential costs. Short-cycle manufacturing (SCM), continuous-flow manufacturing (CFM), and demand-flow manufacturing are some of the competing inventory management techniques (DFM). The JIT inventory system is a departure from the earlier “just- in-case” strategy, in which manufacturers kept significantly bigger inventories of stock and raw materials in case they needed to produce additional units due to increased demand. Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 78 Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes 4.19 BENEFITS OF JIT INVENTORY MANAGEMENT 1. Reduce Wastage Excess inventory and overstocking are eliminated with the JIT inventory management model. Low inventory levels reduce the danger of inventory going unsold and underused in the warehouse. When production quantities are low, the business can also reduce losses due to defective products by quickly identifying and correcting problematic inventory items. 2. Improve Efficiencies Procuring, maintaining, and keeping surplus raw materials and inventories is less expensive using the JIT strategy. This leads to a higher inventory turnover, which avoids products from becoming obsolete by staying in the business warehouse for too long. Additionally, the business can receive and store goods in the lowest quantities feasible, virtually eliminating surplus raw material stockpiles. Local sourcing guarantees that the business suppliers are close to the business manufacturing facilities, allowing for on-time deliveries and lowering the need for safety stock. 3. Increase Productivity JIT inventory management boosts productivity by cutting down on manufacturing time and resources. This means shorter production runs and speedier production. Because there are fewer raw materials on hand, the business can make product adjustments more quickly. Because inventory levels are smaller, product damage is also reduced. 4. Optimize Production JIT inventory management can decrease bottlenecks and delays throughout the whole production cycle by eliminating product defects and automating operations. On-time delivery and increased customer satisfaction are made possible through shorter production cycles. JIT production scheduling guarantees that jobs are planned precisely when they are required, ensuring that the business production runs begin and terminate on time. 5. Reduce Costs JIT inventory decreases working capital requirements for inventory purchases and storage costs by requiring very low inventory levels. The business company can Sorting Inventory and Zone Allocation 79Inventory Management MODULE – 1 Inventory – Introduction and it's Types Notes buy raw materials only when they're needed, allowing it to put its cash to better use. JIT manufacturing also saves money on labour because it requires fewer factory workers than full-time production workers. 6. Improve Quality JIT inventory management entails fewer items on the shop floor at any given moment. This frees up the business management team to concentrate on improving processes and creating high-quality products. High-quality items with fewer flaws improve customer satisfaction and waste. Suppliers ensure quality in the JIT manufacturing model, so deliveries go straight to production and avoid inspection delays. WHAT YOU HA VE LEARNT TERMINAL EXERCISE 1. LEARNING OUTCOMES After completing this lesson the Learner z defines the meaning of sales and operations planning; z states the objectives and features of S&OP; z appraises the process of S& OP; z evaluates the benefits of S& OP. 5 SUPPLY, DEMAND AND OPERATION PLANNING Supply, Demand and Operation Planning 84 Inventory Management MODULE – 2 Role of Inventory Logistics Notes 5.1 MEANING OF SALES AND OPERATION PLANNING The business organization are engaged in save ralactivities from manufacturing products to serving the needs of the consumers. These activities are divided into small units known as departments which are entrusted with the performance of a fixed task. These departments perform these tasks in coordination with other unit departments to achieve the business objectives. For example, to satisfy the needs of the consumers, the business organization has meet the demand of the products through its supply. For this, organizations have to acquire /manufacture products, arrange financing and lastly sell the finished products to the consumers, for which manpower is recruited. Thus, the production department is entrusted with the job of producing products, the purchases department is entrusted with purchasing materials, the finance department arranges for payments and receipt of cash, the sales department is entrusted with the job of meeting the demand for the products by making available the right quantity of products, at the right price, at right time, to right people, at the right place (5 R's). The human resource department is entrusted to hire personnel to perform these activities. 5.2 INTEGRATION OF DEPARTMENTAL TASKS Now, we understand that the overall activity of the organization is divided into specific jobs which are entrusted to departments. Even though the tasks of every department is different and specific, ultimately their outcomes contribute to the achievement of the organization's goal, that is rendering goods to the satisfaction of consumers. That means it is crucial to link the activities of departments for the efficient achievement of organizational goals. This linking is ensured by encouraging departments to perform their activities in collaboration with other departments. Marketing, sales, warehouse, inventory, and product design are significant supply drivers known as supply chain drivers. The management focuses on supply chain drivers to integrate supply to demand by developing Sales and Operation planning (S&OP). Sales and Operation planning (S&OP) is a part of Integrated Business Planning, sales department collaborates with operational department and creates a single long-term production plan. Data relating to the actual performance of the departments are collected and compared for business financial performances and strategies. Sales and Operation planning refers to developing a general plan within the broad frame of a business plan to control inventory flows, its costs and improve its service levels. Sales and Operation planning is a process of coordinating different departments to meet the needs of consumers with adequate quantities of supply. Supply, Demand and Operation Planning 85Inventory Management MODULE – 2 Role of Inventory Logistics Notes INTEXT QUESTIONS 5.1 Match the department to the activities they are concerned about. A (Department) B (Activity) 1. Business (a) Hire Personnel [ ] 2. Management (b) Arrange finance [ ] 3. Sales (c) Serve the needs of consumers [ ] 4. Human Resource (d) Sell products - 5 R's [ ] 5. Finance (e) Integrate Supply to Demand [ ] 5.3 COMPREHENSIVE APPROACH TO SALES AND OPERATIONS PLANNING Sales and Operations planning is the process of developing monthly plans. Its goal is to facilitate the executive manager with the required information for making better decisions by dynamically connecting plans and strategies across the organization. S&OP helps in effective supply chain management by making optimum utilization of the organizational resources to better serve the needs of the consumers profitably. In this globalized, digital era, the development of a dynamic, well connected, and backed by cloud-based technology approach to the S&OP process is essential. Let us now explore a comprehensive approach to the Sales and Operations planning process. To develop an effective S&OP process, six key phases are identified. Fig. 5.1: Approach to S&OP Planning Supply, Demand and Operation Planning 86 Inventory Management MODULE – 2 Role of Inventory Logistics Notes Phase 1: Product Review Phase 2: Demand Review Phase 3: Supply Review Phase 4: Finance Review Phase 5: Pre S&OP Phase 6: Executive S&OP Phase 1: Product Review This is the first phase where need-based products are developed. The Research and Development department analyzes the acceptance of the product in the market, its potential to compete with the already existing products as well as those which are in the pipeline for being launched in the market. The impact of introducing a new product on the already existing products in the market which is known as cannibalization or supersession is also discussed. After reviewing the reports of R&D department, the management decides on the date of introduction of new products, project prioritization, and resource allocations. This requires inputs from departments to evolve an appropriate S&OP to keep abreast of the fast- moving markets. Phase 2: Demand Review Demand for products is forecasted in this phase. Marketing strategies for the new products which are introduced in the market are reviewed. Consumer tastes and preferences, their incomes, along with product hierarchy, and product positioning are some of the factors that are kept in mind while reviewing the demand in the market for a product. The purpose of demand review is to arrive at a consensus demand plan which incorporates both dependent and independent demands. Consensus demand is evolved by combining sales, marketing, and product plans. Demand review leads to the next phase which is supply review. Phase 3: Supply Review A supply plan is developed in synchronization with the consensus demand plan developed in Phase 2. Supply is planned in such a manner as to bring about a balance between maximizing customer satisfaction and minimizing inventory and operational costs. During the supply review, organizational capacity concerning finance, operations, and materials is evaluated by concerned leaders. Constraints of people, machinery, and suppliers are resolved in the supply plan. Supply, Demand and Operation Planning 87Inventory Management MODULE – 2 Role of Inventory Logistics Notes Phase 4: Finance Review Financial commitments of various functional categories viz., product, location, customer, and channels of distribution are reviewed. Actual costs are compared with budgets and the accuracy of forecasting. Financial results of the previous month are consolidated and it is used as a baseline for analyzing the current month's S&OP cycle. The interpretations and conceptions of financial review provide key inputs for pre-S&OP and executive S&OP. Phase 5: Pre-S&OP Series of meetings are organized with different levels of managers to educate them on the connectivity of plans across the products, demand, supply, and finance. The purpose is to identify gaps and disconnects and develop strategies to handle challenges that may emerge during implementation. To gain an understanding of the financial and operational implications of decisions, revenue, profits, and inventory are analyzed against those at the corporate level as well as product line level. Accordingly, real-time adjustments to product, demand and supply plans are made. Phase 6: Executive S&OP Any key issues not addressed in the Five Phases are addressed in this phase. Reasons for escalations are examined and decision deadlines are set. Finally, the objective of S&OP to evolve an aggregate plan is made in this phase. This aggregate plan is sent to various functional heads and in turn, distributed down the hierarchy to all concerned departments. Finally, after making necessary adjustments the consensus plan is approved. INTEXT QUESTIONS 5.2 1.
